Data.MeaningHint: Imagine having your mouth bashed into a gate. It’s going to hurt. Imagine how painful this would be and how problematic it is. Imagine that the only question in your mind is how to get to the hospital as quickly as you possibly can.
Data.ReadingHint: Monsters are scary. Maybe you’ve had a dream where you were being chased by one. Remember how scary that is when you see this kanji.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reason you ran mouth first into this gate was because you were being chased by a <reading>mon</reading>ster (もん). A monster in and of itself is a big enough <kanji>problem</kanji> as is, let alone smashing your mouth into a gate. You poor, hapless soul.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you run into a <radical>gate</radical> <radical>mouth</radical> first, you’re going to have a <kanji>problem</kanji>. Smashing your mouth into a gate is going to hurt. A lot. The problem you’re now in is one of getting to a hospital and having your mouth sewn back on. The <kanji>question</kanji> is, how are you going to get to the hospital?

Data.MeaningHint: Back in the old days a lodge had to be huuuuge so when a leader brought a hundred people through town they'd have a place to stay.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine being someone staying at the lodge as they came in and everything shook around you. This lodge would probably last through an earthquake too!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>lodge</kanji> <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) as the hundred people came tumbling in through the door. It shook, but it didn't fall over. The roof stayed firmly in place, and everyone got to sleep under it okay.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> under which the <radical>leader</radical> can house one <radical>hundred</radical> of his people is at the local <kanji>lodge</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Can you picture a tree without eyes and a heart imagining something? Contemplating? Probably not. To imagine or contemplate, a tree definitely needs eyes to observe and a heart to experience feelings.
Data.ReadingHint: Use your soul to imagine what this tree is imagining with its soul. Is it contemplating the deep mysteries of the universe? Is it imagining where that squirrel has gone off to?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If a <radical>tree</radical> can <kanji>imagine</kanji>, it likely has a <reading>soul</reading> (そう). Its soul is what makes it possible for the tree to <kanji>contemplate</kanji> things, you see?
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Only a <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>eye</radical>s and a <radical>heart</radical> has the ability to <kanji>imagine</kanji> and to <kanji>contemplate</kanji>. For a tree to imagine and contemplate things, it needs eyes to see and a heart to feel.

Data.MeaningHint: The feeling is kind of hard to describe. On the one hand it's warm and loving, but on the other hand it's also kind of wet and slobbery.
Data.ReadingHint: Just be careful not to let in all the Genghis Khan feelings. The confidence and motivation are good, but the feeling of wanting to wantonly slaughter your enemies is probably one you ought to repress.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Overall, though, it's a good <kanji>feeling</kanji> — so good that you feel like Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! With this canine lovingly slobbering on your heart, you feel like you could go out and conquer the world!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>canine</radical> puts its <radical>mouth</radical> on your <radical>heart</radical>, it's a pretty unforgettable <kanji>feeling</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture all the things you've arranged correctly ahead of winter — logs bundled for the fire, blankets for the night, and yourself, all bundled up in your neatly organized home — just as the first snowflakes begin to fall.
Data.ReadingHint: Last winter you tripped and fell on a saber you'd left lying around. Ouch, lesson learned. That's when your passion for organizing things in bundles started. You know better than anyone the value of arranging your sabers as safely and tidily as possible.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: But the most important things to <kanji>arrange</kanji> and <kanji>organize</kanji> are your <reading>sa</reading>bers (せい). You have an extensive saber collection that you simply must organize before winter arrives.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>bundle</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> is the <radical>correct</radical> way to <kanji>arrange</kanji> things. Just make sure you <kanji>organize</kanji> it all before winter comes, or it'll be too late.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a weird sound coming from the sun. Maybe a rumble, or something like a balloon deflating? Then everything goes totally dark. Welp, guess you'll be living in the dark for the rest of your life.
Data.ReadingHint: And because it's so dark, the anchovy taste is all you can concentrate on. Imagine that fishy, salty taste on your tongue, the little softened bones sticking between your teeth. Ugh.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Now wandering around your house in the <kanji>dark</kanji> you start to feel hungry, so you reach blindly for some food and take a bite. Yuck! It's <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん). You don't even like anchovies, how did they get in your house? Hmm… Dark forces are at work here…
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>sun</radical> starts to make a <radical>sound</radical>, you know it's about to go <kanji>dark</kanji>. The sun isn't supposed to make a sound, so if you can hear it, it's probably about to fizzle out or something. After it's gone, obviously everything will be pitch black. Get ready to be in the dark!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine what Sheep-sama must have done to become such important and distinguished sheep royalty. Some pretty amazing things, probably, especially for a sheep.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine playing in the water with Sheep-sama in the summer. It's crazy hot outside in the summer, so going in the water feels nice. Imagine the coolness of the refreshing water envelop your body.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This sheep with the <kanji>formal name ender</kanji> only likes to play outside in the water during the <reading>summer</reading> (さま). Summer is the best season for this fancy sheep. The other seasons just can't compare.
Data.MeaningMnemonic:
By a <radical>tree</radical>, there's a <radical>sheep</radical> in the <radical>water</radical> with a <kanji>formal name ender</kanji>. That means everyone calls him Sheep-sama. No one calls him just "Sheep," since he's kind of a big deal. He's actually like sheep royalty. When he's not in his castle, Sheep-sama enjoys playing in the water by the trees.

This can also mean <kanji>manner</kanji>, as in your manner of doing things. I guess someone with a formal name ender has a different manner of doing things.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture this majestic tree bridge laid on its side, beckoning you into heaven with its mustachioed mouth. "Cross over!" it says in its bridge voice. Do… do you just step over the mouth? Can you wipe your shoes on the mustache, like it's a bristly doormat to heaven?
Data.ReadingHint: Hear the bridge shouting "HASH BROWNS" in its bridge voice. You stuff some hash into its mouth and it starts to chew loudly. Oh, ew… Little hash brown bits are getting stuck in its mustache. Fortunately, the bridge lets you across. Unfortunately, it turns out all of heaven smells like hash browns.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Actually, to get across this <kanji>bridge</kanji> you have to give it some <reading>hash</reading> (はし) browns. That's what the bridge's mouth is for. Hash browns to get across the heaven bridge? Small price to pay, really.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> must've been sent from <radical>heaven</radical>, because it has a <radical>mouth</radical> and a <radical>mustache</radical>. Like some kind of special heaven tree that talks. And why was it sent? To be a <kanji>bridge</kanji>. To be the great heavenly bridge that we all, someday, must cross.

Data.MeaningHint: Still, imagine pulling Wolverine from X-Men out of the water with that thread, and seeing him looking kinda green. Pretty green, but he could be greener. We have to go greener! We have to make the Hulk green with envy! So you put him back in the water, again and again…
Data.ReadingHint: These Marvel crossovers are getting ridiculous.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As the flailing Wolverine begins to turn even more <kanji>green</kanji>, he calls out, "HELP <reading>ME DORY</reading> (みどり)!" Me, Dory? No, not you. He's calling out to that fish from Finding Nemo, another Disney IP. "Help me Dory, I'm green!" Then Dory leaps out of the water to bite through the thread and save Wolverine… THE END. Fade to green.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you tie a <radical>thread</radical> around <radical>Wolverine</radical> and dip him in <radical>water</radical>, he'll turn <kanji>green</kanji>, like the Hulk. Some kind of weird chemical reaction. But I… I wouldn't try it if I were you. You wouldn't like him when he's green.

Data.MeaningHint: You're absolutely obsessed with pointing your thread towards the east, so all you do is practice. Practice, practice, practice.
Data.ReadingHint: Everything in your life is a rental, so you better get to practicing. Your house, your car, your friends — all of these rentals need to be paid off!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You do all of this <kanji>practice</kanji> to try and afford all of your <reading>ren</reading>tals (れん). One day, all of this practice will pay for all of your rentals.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Pointing a <radical>thread</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> is what you <kanji>practice</kanji> every day. You're incredibly passionate about eastward thread-pointing, so you make sure to practice every day.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture all of the people at the temple reciting poems together. You join in too, and it's a really powerful moment!
Data.ReadingHint:
You turn to the person next to you and realize you have to say everything in poem form.

Excuse me
I'm not a creep
so hear my plea
is that a sheep?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you recite a long <kanji>poem</kanji> at the temple you see a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in the crowd. It's opening and closing its mouth like it wants to say the poem too.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> at a <radical>temple</radical> is a <kanji>poem</kanji>. It has to be a poem, because if you say anything else it's just plain disrespectful. All of the monks and priests at this temple have to read and recite poems all day, and if they can do it forever, you can while you're visiting.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine taking a gold coin and scrubbing it with some soap and water. As you scrub, you slowly see that it's really silver underneath. Dang it, silver again!
Data.ReadingHint: Guinea pigs are to silver as normal pigs are to truffles.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If you want to hunt for some <kanji>silver</kanji> for yourself, all you need is a <reading>guin</reading>ea (ぎん) pig. Guinea pigs can smell silver, so get a couple and set them loose in the woods and they'll lead you straight to it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Gold</radical>, at its <radical>root</radical>, is made up of <kanji>silver</kanji>. Using silver as the root of a coin is a great way to save on gold! That's why, often, if you just scratch gold, you'll find there's silver hidden underneath, at its root.

Data.MeaningHint: Everywhere you go, you overhear someone talking about your stylish geoduck-patterned jacket. It's the only topic anyone wants to talk about.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine how annoying it is to constantly be asked about the dye you used in your jacket. You'd really like to change the topic — some might even say you're dye-ing to change it…
Data.ReadingMnemonic: After a few days, the <kanji>topic</kanji> changes from the cool geoduck design to what kind of <reading>dye</reading> (だい) you used to get those real-to-life geoduck colors.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> shines down on your <radical>coat rack</radical> to reveal your <radical>geoduck</radical>-patterned jacket, which then becomes the major <kanji>topic</kanji> of discussion all around town.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a public building, like a museum or a library, and look at the roof. There's a bear eating the roof!
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine Genghis Khan signing something and giving a speech talking about how important it is that all bears are able to eat the roofs of public buildings, as a bear in the background eats the roof.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Public building</kanji>s must make their roofs available for eating because of a law passed by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Eat</radical>ing the <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>bear</radical>, because the roof is a <kanji>public building</kanji>. Because it is a public building bears are allowed to eat the roof.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the horse and the shrimp sitting next to each other on a small bench. There is nobody else at the train station. It is awkward.
Data.ReadingHint: Instead of saying "a key" he says "eh key." How strange. Imagine the station master saying it like this with his weird accent. You ask him if he has "a key" and he replies with, "What is a key?" Then you say "eh key." He totally understands you now.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The train pulls into the <kanji>station</kanji>. They walk up to the train doors, but they don't open. "Perhaps <reading>a key</reading> (えき) would help?" asks the station master, as he goes over to open the door to the train.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> and a <radical>shrimp</radical> are sitting next to each other. They are both at the train <kanji>station</kanji>, waiting for a train to come and take them home.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a hundred million free puppies. That’s a lot. Imagine playing with all of them. You’d be pretty happy. Unless you hate puppies. Then you’d be pretty unhappy, I imagine.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture this cult leader gathering a hundred million people into Oklahoma. It’d be awfully crowded. Imagine yourself being stifled by the bodies of a hundred million other people.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The cult leader has his headquarters in <reading>Ok</reading>lahoma (おく). Are there a <kanji>hundred million</kanji> people in Oklahoma? No, probably not. But hey, this leader is shooting for the stars. First Oklahoma, then the universe!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A cult <radical>leader</radical> has a great <radical>idea</radical> to recruit a <kanji>hundred million</kanji> new members. Since he’s the guy with all the ideas, he is the leader of the cult. His idea is to give every new member a free puppy. He’ll have a hundred million new recruits in no time.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine having all these big products. How will you contain them all!? Imagine yourself stuffing all of these big products into their respective containers.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine the size of these big keys you have to use to lock these containers. It’s a really big key. Picture yourself straining to turn the key in the lock.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Once you get the big products into a <kanji>container</kanji>, you then lock it with a <reading>key</reading> (き). You wouldn’t want these products to bust out of their containers, so it’s best to lock them up with a key.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Big</radical> <radical>products</radical> need to be put in a big <kanji>container</kanji>. A small container just won’t do. These products are big and they need a big container to be contained in.

Data.MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the sheep samurai. Maybe draw him on a piece of paper. He's a sheep wearing wool armor, and he has ram horns on the hilt of his katana.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The most famous <kanji>samurai</kanji> in Japanese history was the illustrious <reading>shee</reading>p (し) samurai. He wasn't JUST a sheep who learned the ways of the sword, he would use the wool from the sheep enemies he killed to create his armor!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>samurai</radical> radical and the <kanji>samurai</kanji> kanji are the same!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine ladling up a nice big scoop of rice and then seeing the little fee ladle next to it. Ugh. The material fee.
Data.ReadingHint: That's a lot of material you have to give up every time you want to eat some rice. Maybe you should take this row boat oar ladle and row out into the ocean where no one can tax you.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: One day, the <kanji>fee</kanji> ladle is suddenly the size of <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat oars! What! You're supposed to pay a row boat sized fee now?!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>rice</radical> <radical>ladle</radical> that's specifically for paying the rice <kanji>fee</kanji>. Each scoop of something has an extra fee tacked on, so when you use a <kanji>material</kanji>, there's a fee you have to pay in that material as well.

Data.MeaningHint: This is not the best signpost you've ever seen, but it's better than nothing at all, you suppose.
Data.ReadingHint: Press it a few more times and hear the sound in your head. Also spell it out in kana as you hear it as a nice little additional visual.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you look at this weird <kanji>signpost</kanji> you notice a button. You press it because it will supposedly tell you more about these dangerous helicopters and jackhammers. Instead, it just yells out in a loud obnoxious voice: <reading>HEYO</reading> (ひょう)!!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>tree</radical> with a <radical>helicopter</radical> and a <radical>jackhammer</radical> tied to it. You look closer, because this is an odd sight, and realize that there is a sign here too. It's telling you to beware of helicopters and jackhammers in these woods. It's a poorly made <kanji>signpost</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: You didn't just cut the tree down, you stabbed it and sliced it and killed it to death.
Data.ReadingHint: Here's that little girl Satsuki again. Remember how you imagined her last time? Try to have a picture of her in your mind so these さつ kanji are easier to remember.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: But after the <kanji>kill</kanji>, <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) appears and yells at you. She likes trees — remember how she protected one in the 札 mnemonic? — so Satsuki's pretty upset you'd kill one like that.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There's <radical>treasure</radical> in this <radical>tree</radical> so you take out your <radical>weapon</radical> and <kanji>kill</kanji> it.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a weird-looking, misshapen crescent moon casting its light onto the scene of a dog boiling up everything you'd find in nature. Trees, sticks, mud, little animals. All of that is being boiled by this dog by the light of a weird-looking moon.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the dog kneeling in front of the pot of concentrated nature and breathing deeply as it practiced Zen. You kind of wish you could join it, considering how peaceful it looks.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Destroying <kanji>nature</kanji> like this is the dog's way of practicing <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) Buddhism. As it breathes in the fumes from all of the different pieces of nature being boiled down, the dog gets really zen and chill.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Under a misshapen crescent <radical>moon</radical>, a <radical>dog</radical> <radical>boil</radical>s as much <kanji>nature</kanji> as it can find.

Data.MeaningHint: Even if you don't have a womb, just picture one anyway: it's turning around in a circle faster and faster, as the heat bubbles and boils in you. You can feel this heat spread through your entire body as you burn up with a fever.
Data.ReadingHint: These nets aren't making the fever heat go away, but they have made the spinning stop at least.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>heat</kanji> from your <kanji>fever</kanji> is making your insides and your head and your entire body spin and boil, until your doctor throws some <reading>nets</reading> (ねつ) around you. You were flailing around and they couldn't get you to stop, so they had to catch you in a series of nets.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>womb</radical> spins in a <radical>circle</radical> and starts to <radical>boil</radical> with <kanji>heat</kanji>. Uh oh, I think you have a <kanji>fever</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Think about it. Every grocery store has the fruit section right at the front, wouldn’t you say?
Data.ReadingHint: Picture yourself driving a car and salivating over the fruit waiting for you in the fruit section. Imagine your favorite fruit as you put the pedal to the metal in your awesome car.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: To get to the fruit <kanji>section</kanji>, you have to go to the store. To get to the store you have to drive a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Driving a car is the fastest way to get to the fruit section at the store.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: I always <radical>say</radical> the <radical>fruit</radical> <kanji>section</kanji> at the grocery should be at the front of the store. It’s the perfect place for the fruit section, I say.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a game at a fair where you have to throw a shellfish into the mouth of a hungry Viking. You want this prize just as much as the Viking wants his shellfish.
Data.ReadingHint: You want this prize. You want to meet the Shougun. Soon you'll both be eating shellfish together!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>prize</kanji> you're working so hard to win is lunch with the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). This is a pretty huge prize, considering all you're doing is knocking something over.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>Viking</radical> opens his <radical>mouth</radical>, and below it, a <radical>shellfish</radical> sits. Grab that shellfish and throw it into his mouth to win a <kanji>prize</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine this wheel you made out of a hat, some ground (presumably to keep it all together), and a bookshelf. You install it on your car and hope for the best. This is why you should buy wheels from professional wheel makers.
Data.ReadingHint: A ring-shaped wheel is better than nothing. Hopefully it will get you where you need to go.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>wheel</kanji> is... generally <reading>rin</reading>g (りん) shaped, so that's good.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: For your <radical>car</radical> you use a <radical>hat</radical>, some <radical>ground</radical> and a <radical>bookshelf</radical> to build a new <kanji>wheel</kanji>. I mean, it's generally <kanji>ring</kanji> and <kanji>loop</kanji> shaped, it just needs to be a <kanji>circle</kanji>, so it should work, right?

Data.MeaningHint: Your angel self says you should choose the safest scooter. The devil self says safety is for suckers, and you should buy one that's loud and dangerous and covered in flames.
Data.ReadingHint: The angel self says, "I choose this centaur scooter because of its strong arms." The devil self says, "I choose this scooter because it looks like a badass horse monster, wooh!"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Suddenly your selfs both gasp and <kanji>choose</kanji> a scooter in front of you that looks like a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). The scooter is designed like a human on the top, the bars are its arms, and the bottom with the seat and the wheels are all horse. This centaur scooter is what you choose to ride.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You want to get a <radical>scooter</radical>, so you have one self (<radical>oneself</radical>) and another self working <radical>together</radical> to <kanji>choose</kanji>. The two tiny yous sit on each of your shoulders, like angel and devil, and argue about which scooter you should choose.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine looking at yourself in the mirror only to see you're made of gold. It would be pretty shocking. Imagine how surprised you would be.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture yourself in front of a mirror, except your reflection is Chairman Kaga Takeshi. (Look up his picture if it helps.) Imagine not recognizing yourself at first, how weird that would feel, then suddenly remembering: "Kaga… me!"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you look into the <kanji>mirror</kanji> you think, "<reading>Kaga</reading>… <reading>me</reading> (かがみ)?" You always forget that you're Chairman Kaga from the Iron Chef TV show. That's why you say "Kaga… me?" every time you look in the mirror.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Gold</radical> reveals itself when you <radical>stand</radical> and <radical>see</radical> into this <kanji>mirror</kanji>. Yes, even you are made of gold. You're so great and valuable, standing in front of that mirror. It's not hard to see, especially in a mirror – you're golden.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine eating a fish fin with sheep wool on it. It tastes fishy like the ocean but the wool tastes like clothes. It's probably the worst thing ever, but try to imagine you're tasting it right now.
Data.ReadingHint: Remember how bad those sheep fins tasted? Now imagine eating your favorite yogurt flavor instead. This yogurt is delicious.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The best way to <kanji>foster</kanji> a better relationship with these foster kids would be to give them some <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Maybe they can dip the sheep fins in the yogurt to help mask the strange taste. Yogurt is also very nutritious, so it's a good supplement to the foster kids' diet.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Sheep</radical> <radical>fins</radical> are <radical>good</radical> for <kanji>foster</kanji> children. The sheep have been genetically combined with fish, so the sheep have fins (and they've lost their tails — which is why the tail part of the sheep radical is missing in this kanji). These sheep fins are rich in nutrients, so they're good for fostering big, strong foster children.

Data.MeaningHint: Start with a blue soul. What feeling is that? Now a red soul. What emotion is that? Go through as many colors as you can think of, focusing on the fact that each color represents a different emotion or feeling.
Data.ReadingHint: Maybe he was just having a bad day, but that still really hurt… and now your soul’s all blue too. Seems you’ll have to walk around with a blue soul for the next few days.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Why is your soul <kanji>feeling</kanji> blue? Because <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big local farmhand, was extremely mean to you.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> is <radical>blue</radical>. As in, it's sad and not feeling well. This is because your soul changes colors depending on your <kanji>feeling</kanji>s or what <kanji>emotion</kanji>s you're expressing.

Data.MeaningHint: When you do a bad thing you should apologize. And saying everyone's private body measurements is definitely something you should not have done. So it's apology time.
Data.ReadingHint: "What do we say?" the shaman says, poking you in the back. "Ugh, I'm sorry I told everyone how tiny your socks are, I guess." "And?" "Annnddd I'll never do it again."
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You were told you had to <kanji>apologize</kanji> by the village <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ). At first you didn't even know why everyone was glaring at you, but the shaman took you aside and explained. And now she's standing behind you with her arms crossed as you apologize to everybody.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You thought it would be a good idea to <radical>say</radical> the <radical>body</radical> <radical>measurement</radical>s of everyone in your village and now you have to <kanji>apologize</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: She's just acting so weird. You doubt she would ever make you hunt for your own meal like this. What are you going to do?
Data.ReadingHint: With her head in the guillotine, it's your mama who starts to look doubtful now. "How could you ever doubt me?" she cries. Ugh, now you're really not sure. This guillotine thing has just compounded your doubts.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Now full of <kanji>doubt</kanji>, you decide to test your mama by putting her in a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). It sounds harsh, but the guillotine is the only way to clear up your doubts.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You reach for a <radical>spoon</radical> to start eating, but your <radical>mama</radical> hands you an <radical>arrow</radical> instead and points towards the <radical>coat rack</radical>. Hm, that's not like her… Suddenly you're filled with <kanji>doubt</kanji>. Is this really your mama? You doubt it.

Data.MeaningHint: Just look at the color white. Compare it to other colors. Now look at white again. Split it up into all the colors, creating a beautiful spectrum. Wow, it's <kanji>all</kanji> the colors!
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine everything covered in white. Everything in the world. Then, you see a dark splotch. It's the miners, they're all covered in coal, ruining all your ambitions. Walk up to them and see all the dirt on the perfectly white uniforms you gave them just a few minutes earlier. Feel enraged at them. How could they ruin all this white you made for <kanji>all</kanji> the people of the world!?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You decide white light is the ultimate color. You decide you want to put it on <kanji>all</kanji> things to make everything white. "What a great idea," you think. You try to put it on everything and you nearly succeed... except, the <reading>miners</reading> (みな). They keep getting dirty doing all their mining things. NOOO, your plan is ruined!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>compare</radical> <radical>white</radical> light to the other colors you'll realize... white consists of <kanji>all</kanji> the colors in one. It contains all the colors in the spectrum in one color. It is the one color that is <kanji>everything</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine that this leader is you. Be rough about it, because 俺 is the "rougher" version of "I." You should only use it in casual situations or if you're a badass.
Data.ReadingHint: More accurately, I'm standing here with an umbrella so I don't get wet. My minions are doing the ore digging, but I am watching to make sure they don't slack off.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Why am <kanji>I</kanji> standing in the middle of this rice paddy with an umbrella? I'm digging for <reading>ore</reading> (おれ).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of a <radical>big</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> is standing in the middle of said rice paddy with an <radical>umbrella</radical>. Who is this wacky leader? Why, it is <kanji>I</kanji>. It is <kanji>me</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a tin can in your mind as clearly as you can.
Data.ReadingHint: かん is can, it's really easy, come on. Just take this one.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
The reading for a <kanji>can</kanji> is かん, which seems impossibly easy. If that isn't close enough for you, picture this:

Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) is drinking out of a tin can. He glug glug glugs, gasps, and wipes his face. He holds out the can, "Khan made cans," and winks.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>can</radical> is the same as the kanji <kanji>can</kanji>. Usually when we talk about a can with this kanji we mean a <kanji>tin can</kanji>, though.
